Note that a ranged attack cannot drop a city below 1 HP; the city must be captured by a melee unit.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_CAPTURINGCITIES_ADV_QUEST: How can a city be captured?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_CAPTURINGCITIES_HEADING3_: When a city's hit points reach "0", an enemy unit may enter the city, regardless of any units already inside. When this occurs, the city is captured. The attacker usually has the option of destroying the city, making it a "puppet," or adding the city to his empire. Whichever he chooses to do, the civilization which loses the city has taken a huge blow. See the City section for more details.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_CITY_HEADING2_BODY: Cities are big, important targets, and if fortified and defended by other units, can be quite difficult to capture. However, doing so can reap rich rewards - in fact, the only way to knock another civilization out of the game is to capture or destroy all of its cities. Do this to enough opponents and you can win a mighty conquest victory (see the section on Victory).
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_CITYCOMBATSTRENGTH_ADV_QU: How does combat strength work for cities?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_CITYFIRINGATTACKERS_ADV_Q: How does the city bombard work?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_CITYFIRINGATTACKERS_HEADI: A city has a Ranged Combat Strength equal to its full Strength at the start of combat, and it has a range of 2. It may attack any one enemy unit within that range. Note that the city's Ranged Combat Strength doesn't decline as the city takes damage; it remains equal to the city's initial Strength until the city is captured.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_CITYHITPOINTS_ADV_QUEST: How do hit points work with a city?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_CITYHITPOINTS_HEADING4_BO: A fully-healthy city has 20 hit points. As it takes damage, the city's hit points are reduced. If a city's hit points reach 0, an enemy unit can capture the city by entering its tile.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_COMBATBONUS_HEADING2_BODY: Units receive a variety of benefits during combat, some from the unit's location, others from its defensive posture, and others from a variety of special circumstances. Some bonuses apply only to an attacking unit, some only to a defending unit, and some might apply to both. The most common bonuses come from the terrain the unit occupies, and whether the defending unit is "fortified."
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_COMBATDAMAGE_ADV_QUEST: How does unit combat damage work?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_COMBATDAMAGE_HEADING2_BOD: A fully healthy unit has 10 "hit points" (HPs). When a unit takes damage during combat it loses HPs, and if it reaches 0 HPs, it is destroyed.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]A unit that has taken damage is weaker than a healthy unit, and it is closer to destruction. Wherever possible, it's a good idea to "rotate out" damaged units from battle to allow them to heal up before reentering the fray. This, of course, is not always possible.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_COMBATRESULTS_ADV_QUEST: How can I determine the results of ranged combat?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_COMBATRESULTS_HEADING3_BO: At the end of ranged combat, the target unit may have sustained no damage, some damage, or it may have been destroyed. Remember that the attacking unit will never suffer any damage during ranged combat (except possibly for air units). If the target is destroyed, the attacking unit does not automatically enter the now-vacant tile (which is what usually happens during melee combat), but you may of course send another unit into the empty space if you've got one with the movement points available.[NEWLINE]The attacking and defending units may receive "experience points" (XPs) as a result of the combat. See the section on "Promotions" for details.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_COMBATSTRENGTH_ADV_QUEST: How does the combat strength unit stat work?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_COMBATSTRENGTH_HEADING3_B: All military units have this stat. Melee units use their Combat strength when attacking or defending. Ranged units use their Combat strength when defending.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_COMBATTABLE_ADV_QUEST: How can I see what will likely happen in combat without starting a battle?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_COMBATTABLE_HEADING3_BODY: When one of your units is active, hover the cursor over an enemy unit to bring up the "Combat Information Table" and learn of the probable outcome of any battle between the two units. This table shows your unit's modified combat strength on the left and your enemy's on the right. The box at the bottom of the screen tells you the likely outcome of the battle, and the bars in the center of the box tell you how much damage each side will take if combat occurs.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_DECLAREWAR_HEADING2_BODY: War against another civ may be declared in a couple of different ways, or you may find yourself on the receiving end of an enemy's own declaration.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_DIPLODECLAREWAR_ADV_QUEST: How do you declare war diplomatically?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_DIPLODECLAREWAR_HEADING3_: You may declare war on a civilization through the Diplomacy panel (see that section). You may declare war on a city-state by clicking on the city-state's city and picking "Declare War" from the pop-up.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_ENDWAR_ADV_QUEST: How can a war be ended?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_ENDWAR_HEADING2_BODY: Wars can end automatically when one side has been destroyed because it has lost its last city. Or the combatants can agree to halt hostilities short of this unpleasant eventuality through diplomatic negotiations. You or your opponent may choose to initiate such discussions. See the section on Diplomacy for details. Barbarians cannot be negotiated with. You'll remain at war with them as long as they're around.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_ENTERTERRITORY_ADV_QUEST: Can you declare war by walking into another civ's territory?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_ENTERTERRITORY_HEADING3_B: It is also an act of war to enter a civ's territory if you don't have an "open borders" agreement with that civ. A pop-up will appear and ask you to confirm your move. Note that it isn't an act of war to cross a city-state's borders, so no pop-up will appear in that case.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_EXPENDING_ADV_QUEST: How and when can I use experience to gain promotions?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_EXPENDING_HEADING3_BODY: When a unit has acquired enough XPs to purchase a promotion, the "Promote Unit" button will flash every time the unit is active. If you click on that button, a list of the promotions available to the unit is displayed. Click on a promotion to choose one. The XPs are expended and the unit acquires the promotion immediately.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_EXPERIENCEPOINTS_ADV_QUES: How do experience points and promotions work?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_EXPERIENCEPOINTS_HEADING2: A unit that survives combat will gain "experience points" (XPs). Once the unit has acquired enough XPs, you may expend them to acquire "Promotions" for that unit. There are a large variety of promotions in Civ V. Each gives a unit special advantages in battle.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_FORITFYBUTTON_HEADING3_BO: If a unit is damaged, the "Fortify Until Healed" button appears in its Action buttons. If you click on this button, the unit will fortify and remain in its present location until it is fully healed. See the section on Fortifications for details of the defensive benefits of fortification.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_FORT_ADV_QUEST: How do forts effect combat results?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_FORT_HEADING3_BODY: Once a civ has acquired the Engineering technology, workers can construct "forts" in friendly or neutral territory. Forts provide a hefty defensive bonus to units occupying them. Forts cannot be constructed in enemy territory. They can be constructed atop resources.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_FORTIFICATION_HEADING3_BO: Many units have the ability to "fortify." This means that the unit "digs in" and creates defensive works in its current location. This gives the unit certain defensive bonuses, making it much tougher to kill. However, fortifications are strictly defensive: if the unit moves or attacks, the fortifications are destroyed. While fortified, a unit will not activate. It will remain inactive until you manually activate it by clicking on the unit.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_FORTIFICATIONBONUS_ADV_QU: How does fortification effect combat results?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_FORTIFICATIONBONUS_HEADIN: The amount of the bonus depends upon the length of time the unit has been fortified. The unit receives a 25% defensive bonus on the first turn it is fortified, and a 50% bonus during all subsequent turns.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_FORTIFY_HEADING4_BODY: Most melee and ranged units can fortify. Non-military, mounted, naval, armored, and air units cannot fortify. These latter units can "Sleep," which means that they will remain inactive until attacked or you manually activate them, but they do not receive the defensive bonus.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_GARRISONINCITIES_ADV_QUES: What does garrisoning a unit in a city do?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_GARRISONINCITIES_HEADING3: A city's owner may "garrison" a military unit inside the city to bolster its defenses. A portion of the garrisoned unit's combat strength is added to the city's strength. The garrisoned unit will take no damage when the city is attacked; however, if the city is captured the garrisoned unit is destroyed.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]A unit stationed in the city may attack surrounding enemy units, but if it does so the city loses its garrison bonus, and, if it's a melee attack, the unit may take damage during the combat as normal.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_GREATGENERALS_HEADING2_BO: Great Generals are "Great People" skilled in the art of warfare. They provide combat bonuses - offensive and defensive bonuses both - to any friendly units within two tiles of their location. A Great General itself is a non-combat unit, so it may be stacked with a combat unit for protection. If an enemy unit ever enters the tile containing a Great General, the General is destroyed.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]A Great General gives a combat bonus of 20% to units in the General's tile and all friendly units within 2 tiles of the General.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Great Generals are created when your units have been in battle and also can be acquired when you unlock the "Warrior Code" social policy. See the section on "Great People" for more details.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_HEADING1_BODY: Combat occurs between two political entities that are at war with each other. A civilization may be at war with another civ or with a city-state. Barbarians are always at war with all civilizations and city-states.[NEWLINE]There are three major forms of combat: melee, ranged, and air combat. The first two occur throughout most of the game, while air combat doesn't happen (naturally) until somebody discovers flight.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Since it occurs so late in the game you needn't be concerned with how to conduct air combat when you begin play.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_HEALINGDAMAGE_ADV_QUEST: How can a city repair damage it has taken?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_HEALINGDAMAGE_HEADING3_BO: A city heals one point of damage every turn, even during combat. Therefore to capture a city the attacker must do more than one point of damage per turn (and usually a lot more than that).
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_HEALTINGDAMAGE_ADV_QUEST: How can a ground unit heal damage it's suffered?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_HEALTINGDAMAGE_HEADING3_B: To heal damage, a unit must remain inactive for a turn. The amount of damage that a unit heals depends upon the unit's location.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]In a City: A unit heals 3 HPs per turn.[NEWLINE]In Friendly Territory: 2 HP per turn.[NEWLINE]In Neutral or Enemy Territory: 1 HP per turn.[NEWLINE]Note that certain promotions will accelerate a unit's healing rate.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_HITPOINTS_ADV_QUEST: How does the hit point unit stat work?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_HITPOINTS_HEADING3_BODY: A unit's health is measured in "Hit Points". When fully healthy, all combat units have 10 hit points. As it takes damage, it loses hit points. If a unit's hit points reach 0, it is destroyed.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_INITIATINGMELEE_ADV_QUEST: How can I initiate melee combat?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_INITIATINGMELEE_HEADING3_: The attacking unit initiates the melee by attempting to move into the enemy's hex. The attacker cannot engage in melee unless it can enter the defender's hex. (In other words, a Spearman cannot engage in melee combat against a trireme since it can't enter that space except when embarked.)[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]To order an active unit to attack, right-click on the target. The active unit will initiate the combat.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_INITIATINGRANGE_ADV_QUEST: How can I initiate ranged combat?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_INITIATINGRANGE_HEADING3_: With the ranged unit active, right-click on the target, and the attack will commence.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_LINEOFSIGHT_ADV_QUEST: How does line of sight work with ranged units?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_LINEOFSIGHT_HEADING3_BODY: Generally, a ranged unit must be able to "see" its target in order to be able to fire at it (with the exception of units with the "Indirect Fire" promotion). A unit cannot see a target if a blocking object is between the two - a mountain or hill, for example, or a forest tile. A unit can always see into a tile, even if it contains blocking terrain, but it cannot see objects in tiles past the blocking terrain. Note that units on hills and flying units can often see over blocking terrain.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_MELEE_HEADING2_BODY: Melee combat occurs when a melee unit (any military unit which doesn't have the Ranged Combat ability) attacks an enemy unit or city. It doesn't matter if the defender has Ranged Combat; as long as the attacker doesn't have Ranged Combat the resulting battle will be melee.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_MELEECOMBATSTRENGTH_HEADI: When two units engage in melee combat, the result is determined by relative strengths of the two units - e.g., if a powerful unit fights a weak one, the powerful unit is likely to do a lot more damage to its enemy, possibly destroying it altogether.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]However many different factors may affect a unit's strength in battle. Many units receive "defensive bonuses" that will increase their melee strength when they are attacked while occupying forests or hills, or are fortified. Some units get bonuses when fighting other specific unit types (spearmen get bonuses when fighting mounted units, for example). Also, a unit's injuries may reduce its current combat strength. (See the section on Combat Bonuses for details.)[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]The Combat Information Table will help you determine the relative strengths of two melee units during your turn.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_MELEERESULTS_ADV_QUEST: How can I tell what happened after melee combat?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_MELEERESULTS_HEADING3_BOD: At the end of melee combat, one or both units may have sustained damage and lost "hit points." If a unit's hit points are reduced to 0, that unit is destroyed. If after melee combat the defending unit has been destroyed and the attacker survives, the attacking unit moves into the defender's hex, capturing any non-military units in that hex. If the defending unit survives, it retains possession of its hex and any other units in the hex.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Most units use up all of their movement when attacking. Some however have the ability to move after combat - if they survive the battle and have movement points left to expend.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Any surviving units involved in the combat will receive "experience points" (XPs), which may be expended to give the unit promotions.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_METHODSOFXP_ADV_QUEST: How can a unit gain experience aside from combat?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_METHODSOFXP_HEADING3_BODY: A unit constructed in a city containing a Barracks or other military building will begin its life with XPs, the number depending upon the specific building. (Barracks and Armories each provide 15 XPs.) Also, certain Social Policies and other special effects may provide XPs to units.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_MULTIPLECOMBAT_ADV_QUEST: How do other units effect melee attacks?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_MULTIPLECOMBAT_HEADING3_B: Units receive a "flanking" attack bonus of 15% for each unit adjacent to the target unit. Some promotions and social policies give an attacking unit additional bonuses beyond the basic flanking bonus. These bonuses can be incredibly powerful when enough units are involved. In general, the more units "ganged up" on the target unit, the better!
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_NAVAL_HEADING2_BODY: Like land units, there are military and non-military naval units. Work boats and any "embarked" land units are destroyed when attacked by barbarians and other civs or city-states.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]All military naval units are ranged combat units. They may attack other naval units and any land units within range that they can see.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Naval combat is resolved like normal ranged combat. There are however certain late-era naval units that merit special attention: the carrier, missile cruiser and the submarines. They are discussed later, in the "Advanced Naval Combat" section.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_NAVALDAMAGE_ADV_QUEST: How can a naval unit heal damage it's suffered?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_NAVALDAMAGE_HEADING3_BODY: Naval units cannot heal unless in Friendly territory, where they heal 2 HPs per turn.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_PROMOTIONSLIST_ADV_QUEST: What are the different promotions available?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_PROMOTIONSLIST_HEADING3_B: There are dozens of promotions available in Civilization V. Some are available to all units, while others can be acquired only by certain unit types. Some promotions require that a unit have acquired other promotions before they become available.[NEWLINE]If a promotion is available to a unit, it will be listed when you click on the "Promote Unit" button.[NEWLINE]See the Civilopedia Section on "Promotions" for more details and for a full list of all promotions.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_RANGE_ADV_QUEST: How does the range unit stat work?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_RANGE_BASICS_ADV_QUEST: How does the range unit stat work?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_RANGE_HEADING3_BODY: Only ranged combat units have this stat. It is the distance, in tiles, within which the ranged combat unit can attack the enemy.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_RANGECOMBATSTRENGTH_HEADI: Any unit that can engage in ranged combat has a Ranged Combat strength statistic. This number is compared with the target's Combat Strength to determine the results of the attack.[NEWLINE]To see the potential effects of a ranged attack, with the attacking unit active hover the cursor over the potential target. The "Combat Information Table" will appear, showing you the losses (if any) the target will take from a ranged attack by the active unit.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_RANGED_HEADING2_BODY: Some units like archers and catapults and triremes engage in Ranged combat (that is, they shoot missiles at enemy units) when attacking rather than engaging in melee combat. Such units have two distinct advantages over melee units: first, they can attack enemy units that are not adjacent to them, and second, they do not take damage when they attack.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_RANGEDSTRENGTH_ADV_QUEST: How does the ranged combat strength unit stat work?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_RANGEDSTRENGTH_HEADING3_B: Only units able to engage in "Ranged Combat" have this stat. It is the ranged unit's combat strength when it is attacking.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_RANGESTAT_HEADING3_BODY: The unit's "range" stat determines the distance at which a unit can launch a ranged attack. A range of "2" means that the target can be in an adjacent tile or one tile distant. A range of "1" would mean that the target had to be adjacent to the attacker. (There are no units with a range of 1, by the way, so don't scour the Civilopedia for 'em.)
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_RECEIVEWAR_ADV_QUEST: Can other civs declare war on me?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_RECEIVEWAR_HEADING3_BODY: At any time another civilization or city-state may declare war on you. If so, you'll be informed by an unpleasant pop-up (or notification). You may have an opportunity to try to negotiate your way out of the conflict, or you may have no choice but to fight. See the Diplomacy section for details. Barbarians are always at war with you, so you'll never get a declaration of war from them.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_SEIGEWEAPONS_HEADING2_BOD: Certain ranged weapons are classified as "siege weapons" - catapults, ballistae, trebuchets, and so forth. These units get combat bonuses when attacking enemy cities. They are extremely vulnerable to melee combat, and should be accompanied by melee units to fend off enemy assault.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Most siege weapons have to be disassembled to move around the map. When they have reached their destination, they must expend a movement point to "set up." They cannot attack until they have done so.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Siege weapons are important. It's really difficult to capture a well-defended city without them!
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_SPECIALCITYCAPTURE_ADV_QU: What are the special rules on capturing cities?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_SPECIALCITYCAPTURE_HEADIN: Naval units and missiles cannot capture a city - although they certainly can soften one up a good deal before the ground unit strolls right in. (And remember that ranged units cannot capture cities either.)
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_TERRAINBONUS_HEADING3_BOD: Defending units get important bonuses for occupying forest, jungle, or hill tiles. Attacking melee units are penalized if they attack an enemy across a river. Attacking units get bonuses when attacking from a hill. See the section on Terrain for more details.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_UNITCOMBATSTATS_ADV_QUEST: What are the unit combat statistics?
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_UNITCOMBATSTATS_HEADING2_: A military unit's combat abilities are determined by its combat statistics. There are four basic combat stats: hit points, combat strength, ranged combat strength, and range.
TXT_KEY_COMBAT_WHICHUNITSFIGHT_HEADING2_: Any military unit may attack an enemy unit. Scout units can initiate combat and defend against enemy attacks, though they are weaker than other units. Non-military units such as workers, settlers, and great people may not initiate attacks. If attacked while on their own, workers and settlers are captured (captured settlers turn into workers) and great people and work boats are destroyed. A city may attack an enemy military unit that is within the city's Ranged Combat Range, and a unit may in turn attack an enemy city.
TXT_KEY_COMMAND_CANCEL_ALL_HELP: This will cancel all queued orders for the currently selected unit.
TXT_KEY_COMMAND_CANCEL_HELP: This will cancel the last order in the unit's mission queue. You will find this to be very useful if you accidentally queue the wrong order.
TXT_KEY_COMMAND_DELETE_HELP: Click on this order to remove the unit from play.
TXT_KEY_COMMAND_GIFT_HELP: This will give the currently selected unit to the player who controls the land the unit is standing on. To give the unit to a different player you must first move the unit into their territory.
TXT_KEY_COMMAND_HOTKEY_HELP: Allows you to assign a hot-key to this unit.
TXT_KEY_COMMAND_STOP_AUTOMATION_HELP: This will cancel the order for the unit to automate itself. It will then rely on you for all orders.
TXT_KEY_COMMAND_WAKE_HELP: This will wake the unit up so that it can be issued orders. Waking a unit does not remove its fortification bonus.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_CITY_STATE_BULLYING_ADVI: How do you ask for tribute?
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_CITY_STATE_BULLYING_SUMM: You may choose to intimidate a city-state with the goal of asking for tribute. Tribute can be in the form of a worker, or a lump-sum of gold. One way to intimidate a city-state is through the size of your military in comparison to other powers, or by having military units close-by.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]If you would like to ask for tribute from a city-state, open the city-state diplomacy panel, and click the "Ask for Tribute" option. If the following options are red, then the city-state is not afraid of you, and will not give in to your demand. If you are successful, you will gain the tribute, but lose influence with the city-state.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_CITY_STATE_MERCANTILE_AD: What does it mean for a city-state to be mercantile?
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_CITY_STATE_MERCANTILE_SU: A mercantile city-state can provide a unique luxury item to your civilization, and help to improve your happiness.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_CITY_STATE_RELIGIOUS_ADV: What does it mean for a city-state to be religious?
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_CITY_STATE_RELIGIOUS_SUM: A religious city-state can provide faith to your civilization.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_CITY_STATE_SPREAD_RELIGI: Is there a benefit to spreading my Religion to a City-State?

If a Religion that you founded becomes the majority Religion in a City-State, the rate at which your Influence degrades is reduced by 25%. Likewise, if your Influence is negative, the rate at which it recovers is increased by 50%.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Additionally, City-States will occassionally ask for a specific Religion to spread to them. If you succeed in accomplishing this, you will receive a significant Influence reward.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_DIPLOMACY_EMBASSY_ADVISO: How do I establish an Embassy?
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_DIPLOMACY_EMBASSY_SUMMAR: Establishing an Embassy is one of the first diplomatic actions available after meeting another civilization, and is available upon researching Writing. Embassies are necessary for trading Open Borders or making Defensive Pacts or Research Agreements, and once set up, will also show you the location of an opposing player's capital. If you have Writing and the other player does not, you can still establish an Embassy in their civilization without reciprocating, although they may ask for a small sum of gold as compensation. Going to war will immediately close down opposing embassies.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_ESPIONAGE_COUNTER_INTEL_: How do I stop other spies?

You can defend against enemy spies by establishing counter-intelligence. To do this, move a spy into one of your cities that you suspect will be a target (usually a city with a high science output). Once there, they have a chance to detect enemy spies. The higher the rank of the spy, the better their chance to detect an enemy spy.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_ESPIONAGE_GETTING_CAUGHT: How do I find out if someone is spying on me?

Spies have a small chance of getting caught whenever they steal technology. If the target city does not contain a counterspy, the possibile outcomes are Undetected, Detected but Unknown, and Identified. If there is a counterspy present, the possible outcomes are Detected but Unknown, Identified, and Identified and Killed. Having your spy either Identified, or Identified and Killed will negatively affect your relationship with the target civilization. If your spy is killed, you will receive a replacement, but not for a few turns.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_ESPIONAGE_GREAT_FIREWALL: How can I slow the progress of an enemy spy?

The Great Firewall Wonder reduces the effectiveness of enemy spies by 99.9% in the city in which it was built, and 25% in all other cities within your civilization. It also negates the [ICON_TOURISM] Tourism bonus from other players' Internet technology.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_ESPIONAGE_MORE_ADVISOR_Q: How do I get more spies?
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_ESPIONAGE_MORE_SUMMARY: Even though you start with only a single spy, you will earn an additional spy as you move into each new era. Additionally, you can earn an extra spy by building the National Intelligence Agency National Wonder.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_ESPIONAGE_MOVING_SPIES_A: How do I move my spies?
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_ESPIONAGE_MOVING_SPIES_S: To move a spy, open the "Espionage Overview" screen, and click the "Move" button next to a spy you want to relocate. After clicking the "Move" button, click on the destination city name on the right side of the screen (either one of yours or a target civilization). The "Activity" for that spy will change to "Travelling", and will indicate how long it will take for the spy to arrive.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_ESPIONAGE_NATIONAL_INTEL: How can I get additional spies?
The National Intelligence Agency National Wonder provides your civilization with an additional spy, and will level-up all your existing spies.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_ESPIONAGE_POLICE_STATION: How can I slow the progress of an enemy spy?

You can slow down enemy spies by building Constabularies and Police Stations in your cities. If a city contains a Constabulary or a Police Station, the time it takes to steal technology is increased by 25% for each.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_ESPIONAGE_PROMOTIONS_SUM: With each successful mission, your spies will gain experience and "level-up". With each successive level, your spies will perform their missions more quickly and reliably.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_ESPIONAGE_RIG_ELECTION_A: How do I use spies with City-States?
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_ESPIONAGE_RIG_ELECTION_S: Spies can be used to increase your influence with City-States by "rigging" their local elections or staging a coup. To interact with a City-State, first move a spy to a target City-State. Once the spy arrives, there will be a period of time when they are "Establishing Surveillance" (noted in the "Activity" column). Once surveillance is established, your espionage options become available. Rigging an election, a passive ability, occurs every 15 turns while your spy is located within the City-State. If you are the only spy in the City-State, your influence will increase while everyone else's will drop. The presence of an enemy spy, especially one of a higher rank, can cause this mission to fail. No worries though, failing to rig an election is not dangerous to your spy.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Staging a coup is more powerful than rigging an election, but also much riskier. A successful coup essentially allows you to "steal" the allied status from another civiization. To stage a coup, target a City-State that is allied with another player, and place your spy the same way you would with rigging an election. Once surveillance is established, you will have a new button available to you called "Coup". Clicking it will open a pop-up message that gives you your odds of success. The closer you are in influence to the target civilization, the greater your odds. The catch? If you fail, your spy is executed.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_ESPIONAGE_SPIES_ADVISOR_: What is a Spy or Diplomat?
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_ESPIONAGE_SPIES_SUMMARY: You will earn your first Spy when any civ in the game enters the Renaissance Era. Spies can be assigned to a city in another civilization with the goal of stealing technology and gathering information. You can also assign a Spy to one of your own cities to have a chance to slow down, discover, or kill an enemy spy.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]When assigning a Spy to the capital of another civilization, you can choose to assign them as a Diplomat. Diplomats do not steal technology, and are only there to gather information. Additionally, if the target civilization is following a different Ideology, your Diplomat will start spreading Propoganda, increasing your [ICON_TOURISM] Tourism bonus in that civilization. Because they are a Diplomat, they are in no danger from other spies. They become even more useful when the World Congress is founded, delivering information on how other civilizations are planning to vote, and allowing votes to be traded via diplomacy.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]To deploy your first Spy or Diplomat, select "Espionage Overview" from within the "Additional Information" menu.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_ESPIONAGE_STEALING_TECHS: Your spies can steal technology from a more advanced civilization. To attempt to steal technology, first, move a spy to a target city within another civilization. Once the spy arrives, there will be a period of time where they are "Establishing Surveillance" (noted in the "Activity" column). Once surveillance is established, you will know whether the target has technology that you can steal. If they do, the time it takes to steal the technology depends on how much science the target city is producing, and the rank of your spy. The higher your rank, the more quickly you will be able to steal technology. The higher the science output, the faster your spy can work.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Other factors that can affect the time it takes to steal technology includes defensive buildings like the Police Station or the Great Firewall wonder, which can stop a spy completely. Additionally, if the target has placed a defensive spy in the same city, your spy can be caught, or even killed (he will be taken out of action for multiple turns). This, of course, will have a negative effect on your diplomatic relations with the target civilization.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_ESPIONAGE_SURVEILLANCE_S: To establish surveillance, move a spy to a target city. Once the spy arrives, there will be a period of time where they are "Establishing Surveillance" (noted in the "Activity" column). Once surveillance is established, you will know whether the target has technology that you can steal. You'll also be able to look inside the enemy city, and have a chance at intercepting important information (like invasion preparations).
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_RELIGION_BELIEFS_SUMMARY: When you found a Pantheon or when you later in the game found or enhance a Religion there are a number of different slots you can fill with a selection of Beliefs. The types of Beliefs you get to select are Pantheon Beliefs, Founder Beliefs, Follower Beliefs and Enhancer Beliefs. There are Civilizations that, due to their unique trait, get to choose an additional Bonus Belief.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Beliefs are picked on a first come first serve basis. When any Belief has been picked it will not be available for any other Civilization which is founding or enhancing their Religion afterwards. This makes being the first to get to this point very valuable.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]A full list of all the different Beliefs can be found in the Religion section of the Civilopedia.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_RELIGION_BUILDINGS_ADVIS: Do buildings play a role with Religion?
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_RELIGION_BUILDINGS_SUMMA: There are two different types of buildings that play a role in Religion. First there are the buildings generating [ICON_PEACE] Faith that you can build the normal way in the city screen once you have researched their prerequisite technologies. But there is a second set of buildings that have to be unlocked through a Follower Belief. They can then be purchased by spending [ICON_PEACE] Faith in any city where the majority of citizens follows the associated Religion.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_RELIGION_DEFENDING_ADVIS: How do I defend against a Religion?
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_RELIGION_DEFENDING_SUMMA: To defend against enemy Missionaries or Great Prophets trying to convert your cities, buy an Inquisitor. These units can be stationed in a city to protect it from conversion. In addition to this passive ability to block competing Religions, Inquisitors can be expended when adjacent to one of your cities to actively remove the presence of all other Religions from your city.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_RELIGION_ENHANCER_SUMMAR: These benefits improve the religious spread. Depending on the individual Belief chosen this can be through more powerful religious units or through stronger natural spread.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_RELIGION_ENHANCING_RELIG: How do I enhance my Religion?

Once you've founded a Religion you can use your next Great Prophet to enhance it. This is done the same way as founding it only this time you will get to choose an additional Follower Belief and an Enhancer Belief.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_RELIGION_FAITH_EARNING_S: [ICON_PEACE] Faith is the currency which enables you to found a Pantheon, makes Great Prophets spawn and can be used to purchase religious units and buildings.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]There are several sources for [ICON_PEACE] Faith points. There are buildings or wonders that award a certain amount of [ICON_PEACE] Faith per round if you build them in your cities. Some Natural Wonders can also generate it when they are being worked. You can find [ICON_PEACE] Faith in Ancient Ruins. If you befriend a religious city-state they will also contribute with regular gifts. And the last source of [ICON_PEACE] Faith is the tile improvement Holy Site that Great Prophets can erect on your lands.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_RELIGION_FAITH_SPENDING_: What can I do with Faith?

[ICON_PEACE] Faith can be spent in several ways. It can be used to purchase religious units or buildings or it will get expended to spawn a Great Prophet, or, once you enter the Industrial Era, another Great Person.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]The [ICON_PEACE] Faith you've accumulated will automatically be spent on spawning a Great Prophet once you've reached a certain threshold (to see this hover your mouse over the [ICON_PEACE] icon in the Status Bar). Once you enter the Industrial Era, you can spend Faith on other Great People as well, depending on which Social Policy branches you've unlocked.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]You can also choose to spend your accumulated [ICON_PEACE] Faith on Units or buildings. This can be done in the Purchase menu of the City Screen. Missionaries and Inquisitors are available in every city that follows a Religion while the respective buildings are only available if the Majority Religion contains a Belief that unlocks the purchase of a Religious Building.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_RELIGION_FOLLOWER_SUMMAR: These attributes are active at the city level. They kick in for any city where this Religion is practiced by a majority of the citizens.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_RELIGION_FOUNDER_SUMMARY: These are player-level bonuses. These positive game effects are received by the player that founded a Religion as long as he still controls the Holy City where the Religion originated. Most of these are directly tied to the spread of the Religion across the map, giving the founder of a Religion a strong incentive to spread his Religion far and wide.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_RELIGION_FOUNDING_RELIGI: How do I found a Religion?

Once you have a Great Prophet and have previously founded a Pantheon you can use the Great Prophet to found a full Religion. This is done by selecting the Great Prophet (make sure he is located in a city), and clicking the "Found Religion" icon on the Unit Panel. You will now be presented with options to choose what sort of Religion you would like for your civilization. Select a symbol and name for your Religion, as well as Founder and Follower Beliefs, which determine what benefits will be awarded. You confirm those choices by clicking on the [ICON_RELIGION] Found Religion button in the lower right of the selection screen.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]There is a limit of how many Religions can be founded in any game, and it's based on map size. That limit will always be lower than the default number of Civilizations in that game, so not everyone gets to found their own Religion.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_RELIGION_MAJORITY_ADVISO: How does a Religion become the Majority Religion?
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_RELIGION_MAJORITY_SUMMAR: Each citizen in a city is either an atheist or follows a Religion. Natural and deliberate spread of a Religion to a city converts them, and once one Religion has a majority of citizens as followers, this city will be considered to follow said religion. At that point, religious units and buildings of that religion can be bought in the city screen.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]If the majority of citizens follows one Religion, the symbol of that Religion will be displayed in the city banner.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_RELIGION_PANTHEON_BELIEF: This is a special type of follower benefit that is especially useful early in the game (usually for improving yields). They kick in for any city where this Pantheon or Religion is practiced by a majority of the citizens. These Beliefs will make their way into main Religions later in the game as some of the Civilizations choose to go beyond a mere Pantheon and found a Religion.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_RELIGION_PANTHEONS_SUMMA: Pantheons are the first step towards founding a full Religion. Once you've accumulated a certain amount of [ICON_PEACE] Faith you will be able to select a Pantheon Belief. The Pantheon Belief you choose will be the basis for any later Religion you might found so choose wisely as it may stay with you for the whole game. The amount of [ICON_PEACE] Faith it takes to found a Pantheon will increase with every Pantheon that has been founded on the whole map.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Pantheons are an early approach to Religion. Consequently it is only possible to found a Pantheon throughout the early ages depending on the developments of Religions around you. Once you've chosen your Pantheon Belief, it will immediately be available throughout your entire civilization, and will automatically expand to new cities until you found a full Religion.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Note that players will no longer be able to found a new Pantheon if another player has already Enhanced their Religion, and the number of active Pantheons matches or exceeds the number of Religions allowable in the current game.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_RELIGION_SPREADING_RELIG: There are two ways that Religion spreads: The natural spread that the holy city as well as any other religious city generates and the deliberate actions a player can perform to convert other cities.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]The natural spread of Religion is fairly straight-forward. Any city that has a majority Religion (more than half of their citizens are following a single Religion) exerts pressure up to 10 hexes from the city. If another city is within that range, and they do not yet have a majority religion, then the pressure will slowly start to convert citizens. If the city already has a majority religion, then it will take two or more cities exerting pressure to start converting citizens. The more cities exerting pressure on the target city, the faster a city's citizens will be converted. You can view your own outward religious pressure, as well as incoming pressure by mousing over the Religion icon on the city banner (if it already has a Pantheon or Religion).[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]To speed up the spread of your Religion the following options are available to you: You can purchase Missionaries by spending [ICON_PEACE] Faith in any city that already has the Religion you wish to spread as its majority. To purchase one just enter the city screen and you can open the purchase menu by clicking on the purchase button in the lower left. The Missionary will be grayed out if you don't have enough [ICON_PEACE] Faith to acquire it. Once you have a Missionary you can move him into or next to the city you wish to convert and click on the Spread Religion button in his action menu which will normally expend him after two uses.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Great Prophets can spread Religion in a similar fashion but additionally they abolish any other Religions that might be present in their target city. They can perform this action multiple times before being expended.
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_RELIGION_UNITS_ADVISOR_Q: Do units play a role in Religion?
TXT_KEY_CONCEPT_RELIGION_UNITS_SUMMARY: There are 3 units that have an impact on Religion: Great Prophets, Missionaries and Inquisitors. While Great Prophets and Missionaries are useful to enhance and spread Religions, Inquisitors defend against unwanted other Religions.
TXT_KEY_CONGRESS_DELEGATES_HEADING2_BODY: Proposed resolutions to the Congress are decided through the use of delegates. Civilizations start with the same number of delegates, but they can gain more by allying with city-states and through certain resolutions. The amount of delegates each civilization controls will also increase as the game advances to later eras.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Once a session of the Congress begins, civilizations divide their delegates as they like between proposed resolutions to support the outcomes they desire. Delegates support "Yea" in order to help pass the resolution, or "Nay" in order to help prevent it from passing. Some resolutions require choosing a civilization rather than simply "Yea" or "Nay".
TXT_KEY_CONGRESS_HEADING2_BODY: The World Congress is a meeting of all the civilizations of the world to propose and decide on resolutions. The Congress is founded by the first civilization that has discovered all other civilizations and has researched Printing Press. The founding civilization becomes the Congress's first host. As the host, that civilization receives special benefits, such as the ability to propose a resolution to the rest of the Congress.
TXT_KEY_CONGRESS_INTRIGUE_HEADING2_BODY: It is possible to find out how other civilizations are planning to assign their delegates. Doing so requires that you either share an Ideology with that civilization, or have a spy as a Diplomat in their capital. The more ways you are able to gather information, the more detailed that information will be.
TXT_KEY_CONGRESS_PROJECTS_HEADING2_BODY: Some resolutions, rather than having immediate effects, begin what is known as an International Project. These resolutions cannot be repealed. International Projects, such as the World's Fair or the International Space Station, are collaborative efforts between all civilizations. Each civilization is able to contribute production towards the project by choosing it in the production list for their cities. Once the project receives enough production from civilizations, it is completed, and large rewards are distributed based on how much each civilization contributed. The highest level reward is always reserved for the civilization that contributed the most. There are also middle and bottom level rewards which are earned by meeting a certain threshold of contribution, depending on the project. Higher level rewards include all the lower level rewards as well, so the civilization that contributed the most will receive the highest, the middle, and the bottom rewards all together.
TXT_KEY_CONGRESS_RESOLUTIONS_HEADING2_BO: A resolution is a change to the game world that is proposed to and decided by the civilizations of the World Congress. For example, a resolution can embargo trade routes with a certain civilization, increase the culture from Wonders, prevent the construction of nuclear weapons, or award someone Diplomatic Victory. A resolution may also repeal a previously passed resolution, reversing its effects. [NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Only two civilizations get to propose resolutions: the current host of the Congress, and the other player who has the most delegates.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]All civilizations get to help decide the outcome of proposed resolutions once the Congress is in session. They do so by allocating their delegates towards the outcome they desire: "Yea" to help it pass or "Nay" to help it fail. Some resolutions require choosing a civilization rather than simply "Yea" or "Nay".[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Once all civilizations have used their delegates, the resolution's outcome is decided by whichever option received the most delegate support. If there is a tie, the resolution fails.
TXT_KEY_CONGRESS_SESSIONS_HEADING2_BODY: A session is when the civilizations of the World Congress decide on one or more proposed resolutions. There are two types of sessions: normal sessions and special sessions. Normal sessions happen on a regular basis, and they can be planned for. The time between these sessions will get shorter as the game goes on, but the number of turns until the session begins is always known.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Special sessions are immediate sessions that happen when civilizations advance into later eras. Once half of the civilizations in the game reach the next appropriate era (or one civilization reaches the era after that), a special session is held which temporarily puts normal session activity on hold. Depending on which era was reached, the number of delegates for civilizations and city-state allies may increase. The only resolution to decide during this special session is who the next host of the World Congress will be. These are the only times that the Congress's host can change (unless the host is conquered, in which case the remaining civilization with the most delegates becomes the new host).
TXT_KEY_CONGRESS_TRADING_ADV_QUEST: How do I trade delegate support?
TXT_KEY_CONGRESS_TRADING_HEADING2_BODY: You can secure additional delegate support for the outcomes you desire before the session begins by trading with other civilizations on the diplomatic trade screen. To allow trading for their delegate support, you must send a spy to their capital as a Diplomat. Civilizations will be less likely to agree to trades if relations are bad or if the resolution goes against their interests.
TXT_KEY_CONGRESS_UN_HEADING2_BODY: Once half of the civilizations reach the Atomic Era (or one civilization reaches the Information Era), the World Congress becomes the United Nations. This unlocks the Elect World Leader resolution, the winner of which will achieve a Diplomatic Victory.
TXT_KEY_CONGRESS_VICTORY_ADV_QUEST: How do I win a Diplomatic Victory?
TXT_KEY_CONGRESS_VICTORY_HEADING2_BODY: Once half of the civilizations reach the Atomic Era (or one civilization reaches the Information Era), the World Congress becomes the United Nations and Diplomatic Victory becomes possible. To win Diplomatic Victory, a civilization must receive enough delegate support on a World Leader resolution.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Unlike other resolutions, the World Leader resolution cannot be proposed by a civilization. Instead, every other session of the Congress is dedicated to deciding it. During these sessions, which alternate with sessions of regular proposals, the World Leader resolution is automatically proposed to the Congress and no other proposals are made.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]The amount of delegate support required to win depends on the number of civilizations and city-states in the game, and can be seen on the World Congress and Victory Progress screens. If no civilization receives enough support to win a World Leader resolution, the two civilizations that received the most support will permanently gain additional delegates.
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_ACQUIRESOCIALPOLICY_ADV_: How is culture tied to social policies?
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_ACQUIRESOCIALPOLICY_HEAD: You acquire social policies based upon the total amount of culture produced by all of your cities. Check out the Status Bar at the top of the Main screen to see how much total culture your civ has accumulated, how much culture the civ is producing each turn, and how much is required before getting a new social policy.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]When you have accumulated enough culture, you can go to the Social Policies Screen and buy a new policy. (See the section on Social Policies for details.) Each time you purchase a new social policy the price of the next one increases.
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_ADV_QUEST: What is culture and how does it work?
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_ANTIQUITY_HEADING2_BODY: To take advantage of an Antiquity Site, you must first build an Archaeologist. Archaeologists are units that can only be constructed in a city with a University. Once built, send him to any Antiquity Site (Open Borders are required for other civilizations), and once they arrive, select the "Construct Archaeological Dig" option on the Unit panel. After a set number of turns, you will have the option to either extract an Artifact, or create a Landmark.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]If you complete the Exploration Social Policy tree, you will also reveal Hidden Antiquity Sites. These special Antiquity sites not only yield up Artifacts like regular Antiquity Sites, but there is also a chance you will find a special Great Work of Writing Artifact, like the Rosetta Stone. You can use this like a Great Work of Writing, or you can choose to start a Cultural Renaissance, which will grant you a large amount of Culture.
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_ARCH_HEADING2_BODY: Once you've researched Archaeology, there will be a new resource that appears on the map called "Antiquity Sites". These are locations that note real events from earlier in your game, like battles that took place on a particular hex, or perhaps a Barbarian camp. To take advantage of these sites, you will need to construct an "Archaeologist" that can extract an Artifact from the site, or build a Landmark.
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_ARTIFACTS_HEADING2_BODY: Artifacts are historical relics that you can place in any open Great Work of Art slot in your Museums, Palace, or certain Wonders. Once placed, they will generate both Culture and Tourism.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]There are also special Written Artifacts, which can only be discovered within Hidden Antiquity Sites (see Antiquity Sites for more information).
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_EXPANDTERRITORY_ADV_QUES: How is culture used to gain territory?
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_EXPANDTERRITORY_HEADING2: As a city gains culture, it will acquire additional tiles in the surrounding unclaimed territory. The faster it gains culture, the faster its territory will grow. Each city acquires territory depending upon its own cultural output. When it reaches a certain level, it will "claim" a new tile (if any are available).[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Check out the Status Box on the City Screen to see how much culture a city is producing each turn, and how long until the city grabs another tile. The amount of culture required to get a new tile increases as the city's territory grows.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Note that you can also expend gold to "purchase" tiles; this is entirely independent of the city's own acquisition based upon its culture.
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_GETTING_ADV_QUEST: How is culture accumulated and what does it give you?
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_GETTING_HEADING2_BODY: Your civilization acquires culture in a variety of ways:[NEWLINE]- Your Palace: Your palace (created when you build your first city) produces 1 culture point per turn.[NEWLINE]- Ancient Ruins: An ancient ruin might give you a big dose of culture.[NEWLINE]- Buildings: Certain buildings provide culture. The monument and temple are two early examples.[NEWLINE]- Specialists: Specialists, particularly artists, generate culture.[NEWLINE]- Wonders: Some wonders churn out culture. The Hermitage, Heroic Epic and National Epics are three national wonders that give you culture, and there are plenty more to discover.[NEWLINE]- Social Policies: Certain social policies will increase your culture output. The "Piety" branch has a number of policies targeted at culture.[NEWLINE]- Great Artist: A Great Artist can construct a "Landmark" improvement in a tile. If your city works that tile, it will gain a lot of culture.[NEWLINE]- City-States: Some city-states give you culture if your relations are friendly.
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_GREAT_WORK_ART_ADV_QUEST: What is a Great Work of Art?
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_GREAT_WORK_ART_HEADING2_: Great Artists can create a Great Work of Art to place in any appropriate building in your empire that has an empty Great Work of Art slot. This includes the Palace, Museums, Cathedrals, as well as various Wonders.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]To create a Great Work of Art, select and move your Great Artist to within one hex of any city in your civilization. As long as there is an appropriate building with an empty slot available in any of your cities, you will be able to click the "Create Great Work" icon on the Unit Panel. The Great Work will display on your screen (for example, Leonardo Da Vinci's Mona Lisa), and the Great Artist is expended.
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_GREAT_WORK_MUSIC_ADV_QUE: What is a Great Work of Music?
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_GREAT_WORK_MUSIC_HEADING: Great Musicians can create a Great Work of Music to place in any appropriate building in your empire that has an empty Great Work of Music slot. This includes the Opera House, the Broadcast Tower, as well as various Wonders.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]To create a Great Work of Music, select and move your Great Musician to within one hex of any city in your civilization. As long as there is an appropriate building with an empty slot available in any of your cities, you will be able to click the "Create Great Work" icon on the Unit Panel. The Great Work of Music will display on your screen (for example, Frederic Chopin's Raindrop Prelude), and the Great Musician is expended.
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_GREAT_WORK_WRITING_ADV_Q: What is a Great Work of Writing?
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_GREAT_WORK_WRITING_HEADI: Great Writers can create a Great Work of Writing to place in any appropriate building in your empire that has an empty Great Work of Writing slot. This includes the Amphitheater, the National Epic, the Heroic Epic, the Royal Library, as well as various Wonders.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]To create a Great Work of Writing, select and move your Great Writer to within one hex of any city in your civilization. As long as there is an appropriate building with an empty slot available in any of your cities, you will be able to click the "Create Great Work" icon on the Unit Panel. The Great Work of Writing will display on your screen (for example, Charles Dickens' A Christmas Carol), and the Great Writer is expended.
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_GREAT_WORKS_HEADING2_BOD: Great Artists, Writers, and Musicians all have the ability to create Great Works. These Great Works are iconic masterpieces from throughout time, like Leonardo Da Vinci's Mona Lisa, or Charles Dickens' A Christmas Carol.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Once created, you can then place these Great Works in an appropriate building that has an empty Great Work slot (like a Museum for a Great Work of Art, an Amphitheater for a Great Work of Writing, or an Opera House for a Great Work of Music). Once placed, each Great Work produces both Culture and Tourism.
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_HEADING1_BODY: Culture is a measurement of your civilization's commitment to and appreciation of the arts and humanities - everything from cave paintings to Tiki heads to "Hamlet" to Beethoven's Ninth Symphony to Lady Gaga's latest video. In the game culture has two main effects: it increases the size of your cities' territories (and therefore your overall borders) and it allows you to purchase new "Social Policies." Most importantly, if you acquire enough culture you can win a "cultural victory."
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_MANAGE_GREAT_WORKS_ADV_Q: How do I manage my Great Works?
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_MANAGE_GREAT_WORKS_HEADI: At any time, you can open the Cultural Overview screen, and move Great Works from one location to another. This is helpful when your civilization is being invaded and you wish to move your Great Works to safety. You can also use this to place Great Works in different combinations within your Museums and Wonders to attempt a theming bonus, which gives you bonus [ICON_TOURISM] Tourism for combining similar Great Works.
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_TOURISM_AND_CULTURE_ADV_: How do I win using Tourism?
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_TOURISM_AND_CULTURE_HEAD: When your cumulative [ICON_TOURISM] Tourism output (for the entire game) surpasses the cumulative Culture output of each remaining civilization in the game, you win the Culture Victory. In terms of a Culture Victory, consider Tourism your "Offense" against other civilizations, and Culture your "Defense" from other civilizations. You can track your progress by clicking on the [ICON_TOURISM] Tourism icon at the top of the screen.
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_TOURISM_BENEFITS_ADV_QUE: What are the benefits of Tourism?
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_TOURISM_BENEFITS_HEADING: Aside from accumulating [ICON_TOURISM] Tourism to win a Culture Victory, there are other benefits to be gained from accumulating [ICON_TOURISM] Tourism. The more [ICON_TOURISM] Tourism you generate, the greater your Influence becomes with other civilizations (you can view your progress in the "Influence by Player" tab of the "Cultural Overview"). Once you've reach the "Familiar" level with another civilization, you will start receiving bonuses that grow as your Influence grows. These include additional [ICON_RESEARCH] Science from Trade Routes, more effective [ICON_SPY] Spies, and easier control of conquered cities (less unrest, and more population remaining).[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Ideology choices are also affected. If another civilization has chosen a different Ideology than your civilization, gaining Influence over their people will cause unhappiness within their civilization, forcing them to either cope with their unhappy people, or change Ideologies. For more information, see the "Social Policies" Game Concepts section, and click on "Ideologies".
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_TOURISM_HEADING2_BODY: [ICON_TOURISM] Tourism is generated by Great Works or Artifacts that are placed in certain Culture buildings, and is the primary yield you will use to spread your cultural influence to other civilizations. As you place more and more Great Works and Artifacts into your Museums, Amphitheaters, Opera Houses, etc., your [ICON_TOURISM] Tourism will continue to increase. Additionally, you can boost your [ICON_TOURISM] Tourism output quite a bit through Open Borders, Trade Routes, shared Religion, and certain Ideological Tenets.
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_VICTORY_ADV_QUEST: How does culture lead to cultural victory?
TXT_KEY_CULTURE_VICTORY_HEADING2_BODY: Remember that you can achieve victory by acquiring enough social policies and then constructing the "Utopia Project." See the section on Victory for details.
TXT_KEY_CW_MAP_PACK_AFRICA_DESC: A map of Africa, randomized except for some key features.
TXT_KEY_CW_MAP_PACK_ANTARCTICA_THAWED_DE: A randomized fantasy version of Antarctica where only a small portion remains frozen.
TXT_KEY_CW_MAP_PACK_AUSTRALIA_DESC: A map of Australia, with randomized terrain and coastal start locations.
TXT_KEY_CW_MAP_PACK_CANADA_DESC: A map of Canada with northern banding of tundra and snow, and randomly generated swaths of plains, grasslands and forest.
TXT_KEY_CW_MAP_PACK_CHINA_DESC: A script-driven map that resembles the shape and terrain of China, completely randomized.
TXT_KEY_CW_MAP_PACK_EARTH_DESC: The entire Earth, with geographically correct landforms and randomized terrain and features.
TXT_KEY_CW_MAP_PACK_EAST_ASIA_DESC: A map of Eastern Asia, where the landforms and the Himalayas are constant, with all other terrain randomized.
TXT_KEY_CW_MAP_PACK_FRANCE_DESC: A map of France, where the landform is constant, and all internal terrain is randomized.
TXT_KEY_CW_MAP_PACK_ITALY_DESC: A map of Italy, where the landform and the Alps are constant, with all other terrain randomized.
TXT_KEY_CW_MAP_PACK_JAPAN_DESC: A map of Japan, where the landform and key mountains are constant, and all other terrain is randomized.
TXT_KEY_CW_MAP_PACK_MIDDLE_EAST_DESC: A map of the Middle East spanning the Black Sea to the Arabian Sea, where the landforms are constant, and all other terrain is randomized.
TXT_KEY_CW_MAP_PACK_NORTH_AMERICA_DESC: A map of North America spanning Colombia to Alaska, where the landforms and northern tundra are constant, and all other terrain and features are randomized.
TXT_KEY_CW_MAP_PACK_OCEANIA_DESC: Similar to Archipelago, with more of a continental feel with clusters of broken continents, small islands, and plenty of open water.
TXT_KEY_CW_MAP_PACK_RUSSIA_DESC: A map of Russia, where the landform is constant, and all the internal terrain is randomized.
TXT_KEY_CW_MAP_PACK_SCAN_DESC: A map of Scandinavia, randomized except for some key features.
TXT_KEY_CW_MAP_PACK_SMALL_CONT_PLUS_DESC: A new approach to the original Small Continents map, adding sprawling island chains and better continent generation.
TXT_KEY_CW_MAP_PACK_SOUTH_AMERICA_DESC: A map of South America, where the landform is constant, and all other terrain and features randomized, including the "wandering" mountain ranges.
TXT_KEY_CW_MAP_PACK_TURKEY_DESC: A map of Turkey, where the landform is constant, and all internal terrain is mountainous and randomized.
TXT_KEY_CW_MAP_PACK_UK_DESC: A map of the United Kingdom, including England, Scotland and Northern Ireland, with all terrain randomized.
TXT_KEY_CW_MAP_PACK_WESTERN_EUROPE_DESC: A map of Western Europe, where the landforms and mountain ranges are constant, with all other terrain and features randomized.
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_DECLAREWAR_ADV_QUEST: How can you declare war during diplomacy?
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_DECLAREWAR_HEADING3_BO: Click on this button to declare war with the civilization.
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_DECLARINGWAR_ADV_QUEST: How can you declare war on a civilization or city-state?
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_DECLARINGWAR_HEADING2_: You can declare war on a city-state or another civilization through the Diplomacy Panel or by simply attacking one of their units. You can declare war on a civ by entering their territory without an Open Borders agreement, as well (except for city-states, whose borders you may enter freely). Other civilizations can declare war on you in the same fashion.
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_DEFENSIVEPACT_ADV_QUES: How does a defensive pact work?
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_DEFENSIVEPACT_HEADING3: Once you have acquired the Chivalry tech, you may engage in a Defensive Pact. Defensive Pacts are always mutual. If a signatory to a Defensive Pact is attacked, the other partner is automatically at war with the attacker.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]A Defensive Pact lasts for 30 turns (on standard speed). When that time has elapsed, the pact lapses unless it is renegotiated. The Defensive Pact is nullified if one of the participants declares war on anybody.
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_DEMAND_ADV_QUEST: Can you make demands of other civilizations?
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_DEMAND_HEADING3_BODY: You can demand stuff from the other civ. It may comply if you're a lot bigger than it or if it otherwise feels it's appropriate. Or it may declare war on you. You never know...
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_DENOUNCE_HEADING3_BODY: Players have the ability to publicly "Denounce" other Civs who have slighted them. However, there are diplomatic repercussions to consider and denunciations shouldn't be taken lightly. A Civ you Denounce will be wary of any later attempts at diplomacy, and it will take years before they forget your actions. On the other hand, a carefully timed denunciation as a prelude to war could benefit you by drawing the other enemies of your target out of the woodwork and into the war as your allies.
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_DISCUSS_ADV_QUEST: How can I make informal requests to other civilizations?
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_DISCUSS_HEADING3_BODY: This button allows you to open up dialog on a variety of topics. Depending upon circumstances you may do any of the following. The leader's response will depend upon his or her relations with you and their own self-interest.[NEWLINE]- Ask the leader to sign a declaration of friendship.[NEWLINE]- Ask the leader to go to war against another civilization.[NEWLINE]- Request that the leader not build any more new cities near you.[NEWLINE]- Denounce a leader you are unhappy with.
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_EXIT_ADV_QUEST: How can I leave the diplomacy screen?
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_EXIT_HEADING3_BODY: Press this to exit diplomacy with the leader.
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_FRIENDSHIP_HEADING3_BO: A "Declaration of Friendship" between two or more players means, in the most basic sense that these players now have a mutual interest in looking out for each other (typically this involves becoming trade partners and sharing resources). Players may also choose to establish "blocks" of friendship by forming friendships with multiple players in order to ensure long-term safety and economic stability.
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_HEADING1_BODY: Diplomacy is important in Civilization V. The world is huge and filled with other civilizations whose leaders are just as cunning and determined as you are. Some are honest; others are liars. Some are warlike and others prefer peace. But all want to win.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]You can accomplish a lot through diplomacy. You can gain allies and isolate your enemies. You can create defensive and offensive pacts. You can increase your technology through cooperative research ventures. You can end wars that are going badly for you. You can bluff the credulous and bully the timid.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]It's a big, tough world out there, and you won't last long if you automatically attack everybody you meet. Sometimes it really is better to talk than to fight - at least until their back is turned and you're ready to launch the big attack.
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_INITIATING_HEADING2_BO: To initiate diplomacy, click on the Diplomacy Panel button. The Diplomacy Panel will appear, displaying all known civilizations and city-states in the game. Click on an entry to speak with that leader. Alternatively, you can click on a civ's or city-state's city banner to open communications with them.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]What you can accomplish depends upon whether you're speaking with a civilization or a city-state.
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_NEGOTIATEPEACE_ADV_QUE: How can you negotiate peace with a civilization or city state?
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_NEGOTIATEPEACE_HEADING: While at war, you can offer to negotiate peace through the Diplomacy Panel. Your opponent may refuse to negotiate altogether, in which case the war will continue.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]If it's willing to negotiate at all, a city-state will always accept an offer of peace without pre-conditions.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]If your opponent is a civilization who is willing to discuss peace, you may negotiate the price for peace on the Trade Table. Depending upon circumstances one side or the other may give the opponent gold, treaties, cities, and/or resources in exchange for peace.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Note that your opponent can also offer to negotiate peace. It's usually a good idea to at least see what they're offering before deciding upon your response.
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_NEGOTIATING_ADV_QUEST: How do you conduct diplomacy with city-states?
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_NEGOTIATING_HEADING2_B: City-States are much less complicated than civilizations. You have fewer options when negotiating with them. Generally, you can offer them gold or goods, or declare war or offer peace. Sometimes they will ask favors of you. See the section on City-States for more details.
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_OPENBORDERS_ADV_QUEST: How does the open borders agreement work?
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_OPENBORDERS_HEADING3_B: Once you or another civ have discovered Writing, you can enter into an Open Borders agreement with that civ. (City-States can't make Open Borders agreements.) While an Open Borders agreement is in effect, the other civ's units can enter your territory without automatically triggering war. If the agreement is mutual, either civ's units can enter the other's territory freely; however it doesn't have to be mutual: one civ can grant another Open Borders without automatically receiving it in return.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]An Open Borders agreement lasts for 30 turns (on standard speed). When 30 turns have passed, the agreement must be renegotiated or it lapses.
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_OTHERPLAYERS_ADV_QUEST: Can you ask another civilization to declare war or offer peace to another civilization?
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_OTHERPLAYERS_HEADING3_: You can ask your trading partner to interact with other civs that you both know. You can ask him or her to declare war or make peace with another player.
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_PEACE_ADV_QUEST: When can you negotiate peace with a civilization?
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_PEACE_HEADING3_BODY: If you're at war with the civ, you can discuss peace.
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_RESEARCHAGREE_HEADING3: Once you have acquired the Philosophy tech, you may engage in a Research Agreement with another civ. A research agreement costs each side gold (if you don't have the required gold, you can't be part of an agreement).[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]The Research Agreement lasts 20 turns. After 20 turns you gain research equal to half the average cost of the techs available to you.
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_RESOURCES_HEADING3_ADV: Can you trade resources with another civilization?
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_RESOURCES_HEADING3_BOD: You can trade Strategic and Luxury resources with another civ. The other civ gets all of the benefits of the resource for the duration of the trade (30 turns).
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_TRADE_ADV_QUEST: What does trade mean in diplomacy?
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_TRADE_HEADING3_BODY: You can negotiate a trade deal with the civilization. Clicking on this button will bring up the Trade Screen.
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_TRADECITIES_ADV_QUEST: Can you trade cities with other civilizations?
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_TRADECITIES_HEADING3_B: You can trade cities with other civilizations. Generally, civilizations will not trade cities unless in dire circumstances or in exchange for huge payouts. You cannot trade your capital city.
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_TRADESCREEN_ADV_QUEST: How does the trade screen work?
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_TRADESCREEN_HEADING2_B: The Trade Screen allows you to trade items, to make research agreements and to enter into other kinds of treaties. Many options require knowledge of certain technologies before you use them. If you cannot trade something, it is greyed out. Hover your cursor over a line to learn more about it.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]The Trade Screen is divided into two sides. Your civilization's stuff is on the right side, and the other civilization's is on the left.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Click on items on your side to offer them to your trading partner; click on items on his or her side to indicate what you want in return. You might, for example, offer your opposite number "Open Borders" (permission for his units to enter your territory) in return for "Open Borders" (permission for your units to enter his territory).[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]However, trades do not need to be equal: you can, for example, ask for "Open Borders" in return for gold, or nothing. Once you've set up the trade you want, click on the "Propose" button to present it to the other civilization. If the other civilization accepts the offer, it goes into effect immediately. If the other civilization rejects it, you click on "What would make this deal work?" to ask what the leader wants. (Note: there are times when the CPU will never give up a certain item, no matter how tempting your offer is.)[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Sometimes the other leader will make you an offer. You can accept the offer or make a counter-offer or decline it altogether.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Click on the "Exit" button to leave this screen.
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_WHOCANCONDUCT_HEADING2: You can speak to a city-state or another civilization's leader at any time after you've established diplomatic relations with them. This happens automatically when one of your cities or units encounters one of their cities or units. (In fact, the desire to establish diplomatic relations is one of the driving forces behind world exploration.) After you've established diplomatic relations with another political entity you can speak with them at any time - though they may not have much to say if they hate your guts.[NEWLINE][NEWLINE]Note that another civilization or city-state may attempt to open negotiations with you, as well, after you've opened diplomatic relations.
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_WITHCIVS_ADV_QUEST: What are the diplomatic options when dealing with a civilization?
TXT_KEY_DIPLOMACY_WITHCIVS_HEADING2_BODY: When you engage in diplomacy with civilizations, you have the following options: declare war, negotiate peace, trade, demand, discuss, and exit.
TXT_KEY_DQ_LEADER_NAME_1_QUOTE: I found Rome a city of bricks and left it a city of marble.
TXT_KEY_DQ_LEADER_NAME_10_QUOTE: Judgement comes from experience, and experience comes from bad judgement.
TXT_KEY_DQ_LEADER_NAME_11_QUOTE: He who puts out his hand to stop the wheel of history will have his fingers crushed.
TXT_KEY_DQ_LEADER_NAME_12_QUOTE: Withal, I concern myself greatly regarding the affairs of state, constant wars against hostile nations, and the welfare of my poor people.
TXT_KEY_DQ_LEADER_NAME_13_QUOTE: My Lord, if it were not to satisfy the world, and my Realm, I would not do that I must do this day for none earthly thing.
TXT_KEY_DQ_LEADER_NAME_14_QUOTE: Blessed are the young for they shall inherit the national debt.
TXT_KEY_DQ_LEADER_NAME_15_QUOTE: Listeners never hear any good of themselves.
TXT_KEY_DQ_LEADER_NAME_16_QUOTE: In war, whichever side may call itself the victor, there are no winners, but all are losers.
TXT_KEY_DQ_LEADER_NAME_17_QUOTE: There is no pleasure in having nothing to do; the fun is having lots to do and not doing it.
TXT_KEY_DQ_LEADER_NAME_18_QUOTE: So great an artist, I die!
TXT_KEY_DQ_LEADER_NAME_19_QUOTE: Somewhere there must be a book that tells all about it, where I could go to straighten it out in my mind. But I don't know where the book is, and maybe I couldn't read it if I found it.
TXT_KEY_DQ_LEADER_NAME_2_QUOTE: When Marduk sent me to rule over men, to give the protection of right to the land, I did right and righteousness, and brought about the well-being of the oppressed.
TXT_KEY_DQ_LEADER_NAME_20_QUOTE: Yeah, just five minutes, all right?
TXT_KEY_DQ_LEADER_NAME_21_QUOTE: If we don't succeed, we run the risk of failure.
TXT_KEY_DQ_LEADER_NAME_3_QUOTE: Always bear in mind that your own resolution to succeed is more important than any other.
TXT_KEY_DQ_LEADER_NAME_4_QUOTE: Let my armies be the rocks and the trees, and the birds in the sky.
TXT_KEY_DQ_LEADER_NAME_5_QUOTE: A pessimist sees the difficulty in every opportunity; an optimist sees the opportunity in every difficulty.
TXT_KEY_DQ_LEADER_NAME_6_QUOTE: It always seems impossible until it's done.
TXT_KEY_DQ_LEADER_NAME_7_QUOTE: Because a thing seems difficult for you, do not think it impossible for anyone to accomplish.
TXT_KEY_DQ_LEADER_NAME_8_QUOTE: I am not afraid... I was born to do this.
TXT_KEY_DQ_LEADER_NAME_9_QUOTE: Faced with crisis, the man of character falls back on himself. He imposes his own stamp of action, takes responsibility for it, makes it his own.
TXT_KEY_EA67AED558594875BF3A360FE9E55D1B: Conquest of the New World Deluxe
TXT_KEY_ECONOMICAISTRATEGY_CITIES_NEED_N: We have a lot of coastal cities so we should focus on buildings that improve yields from our coastal tiles.
We have water resources that we're not using. Build workboats and deploy them to help our civilization!
TXT_KEY_ECONOMICAISTRATEGY_EARLY_EXPANSI: We have room to expand, so let's train a settler and found a new city!
TXT_KEY_ECONOMICAISTRATEGY_ENOUGH_EXPANS: We should focus on growing and improving what we have rather than founding new cities right now.
TXT_KEY_ECONOMICAISTRATEGY_ENOUGH_RECON: We have enough explorers to discover the world. We should not start building any new land exploration units.
TXT_KEY_ECONOMICAISTRATEGY_FOUND_CITY: We have a settler, now find a good spot to found a city!
TXT_KEY_ECONOMICAISTRATEGY_HALT_GROWTH_B: We're working on the Great Pyramid, so we needn't build granaries in our other cities.
TXT_KEY_ECONOMICAISTRATEGY_ISLAND_START: Our homeland is a small continent without much room for expansion. We need to focus on naval technologies until we can embark units.
TXT_KEY_ECONOMICAISTRATEGY_LOSING_MONEY: We need to increase our [ICON_GOLD]gold income to stay competitive. Disband obsolete units to lower our unit maintenance, use our workers to construct improvements that increase the amount of [ICON_GOLD]gold from a tile, and construct buildings that increase our [ICON_GOLD]gold income.
TXT_KEY_ECONOMICAISTRATEGY_NEED_HAPPINES: Our people's [ICON_HAPPINESS_1] happiness is becoming a concern. We need to connect luxury resources, build [ICON_HAPPINESS_1]happiness buildings and wonders, and adopt policies that help make our civilization happy!
Our people are quite Unhappy! We need to connect luxury resources, build [ICON_HAPPINESS_1]happiness buildings and wonders, and adopt policies that help make our civilization happy quickly!
TXT_KEY_ECONOMICAISTRATEGY_NEED_IMPROVEM: We are having a [ICON_FOOD]food crisis! Use Workers to build improvements that increase the amount of [ICON_FOOD]food that a plot provides.
We are having a [ICON_PRODUCTION]production crisis! Use Workers to build improvements that increase the amount of [ICON_PRODUCTION]production that a plot provides.
TXT_KEY_ECONOMICAISTRATEGY_NEED_RECON: We should continue exploring the world to discover ancient ruins, natural wonders, and other civilizations!
TXT_KEY_ECONOMICAISTRATEGY_NEED_RECON_SE: We need to continue exploring the seas to find new continents to settle.
TXT_KEY_ECONOMICAISTRATEGY_TOO_MANY_UNIT: Our [ICON_PRODUCTION]production is being diminished by the number of units that we have. We should disband any unneeded units so that our civilization can operate at full capacity.
TXT_KEY_FEATURE_FALLOUT_PEDIA: Fallout is the residual radiation left over following a nuclear explosion.
